Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare

or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Let $w_1, \ldots, w_m$ be non-negative real numbers such that $\sum_{i=1}^m w_i=1$, and let 
$v_1, \ldots, v_m$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli$(p)$ random variables. For which values of probability $p$ is it true that
\[\Pr\Big[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\geq p\Big]\geq p?\]

This run is resumed from an earlier workflow that terminated at round 15. The new upper round bound is round 20; continue the existing draft and round history instead of restarting the solution.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on the revised draft

### Executive verdict

The revised `answer.tex` is still **not a complete solution** of the stated problem. The main positive assertion for all \(0\le p\le 1/3\) remains explicitly labeled as **Conjecture~\(\ref{conj:missing}\)** and is repeatedly described as an “essential missing assertion.” The newly added centered two-tail comparison formulation is a valid reformulation of the same missing assertion, but it does not prove it.

Therefore the answer remains a partial-resolution/reduction document, not an answer-ready proof.

---

## LaTeX contract audit

From the source:

- It uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- It uses `fullpage`, which is permitted.
- I see no forbidden packages such as `geometry`, no manual margin changes, no line-spacing commands, and no explicit in-document font-size changes such as `\small`, `\footnotesize`, `\scriptsize`, or `\fontsize`.
- `answer.tex` is standalone and contains a manual `thebibliography`, so `references.bib` is not needed for compilation.
- The author reports successful `pdflatex` compilation and 11 pages. I see no source-level compile blocker.

Thus the LaTeX contract does not appear to be the reason for rejection. The reason is mathematical incompleteness.

---

## Literature validation

The Fokkink–Meester–Pelekis citation is real and accurately described at a high level: their paper studies maximizing \(\Pr(S_\gamma\ge t)\) over convex combinations of iid Bernoulli variables and defines the function \(\pi(p,t)\). ([alea.impa.br](https://alea.impa.br/articles/v20/20-07.pdf)) The draft’s claim that their Theorem 4.4 covers certain rectangular regions, not the full near-hypotenuse region needed here, is consistent with the stated theorem \(k/(k+1)<p\le (k+1)/(k+2)<t\). ([alea.impa.br](https://alea.impa.br/articles/v20/20-07.pdf)) Their Proposition 4.5 treats boundary points \(p=t=(k+1)/(k+2)\), again not the whole missing strip. ([alea.impa.br](https://alea.impa.br/articles/v20/20-07.pdf))

The Frankl citation is also bibliographically correct: *On Sperner families satisfying an additional condition*, Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series A 20, issue 1, pages 1–11, 1976. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/009731657690073X?utm_source=openai))

No cited literature in the revised `answer.tex` supplies the missing proof.

---

## What changed since the previous review

The main addition is the centered two-tail comparison formulation:

\[
Y=\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p),\qquad 0\le b_i\le1,
\]
and the claimed equivalent inequality

\[
q\,\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge p\,\Pr[Y<-q]. \tag{CTC}
\]

This reformulation is correct: after scaling \(h=1\), HUG says

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\ge p(B+1)\right]
\ge \frac pq
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i<p(B+1)-1\right],
\]

and subtracting \(pB\) from the sum gives precisely \(Y\ge p\) and \(Y<-q\). Multiplying by \(q\) gives (CTC). However, this is only another equivalent form of the unproved conjecture. It does not close the gap.

The previous central objection therefore remains fully unresolved.

---

## Section-by-section mathematical audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation is clear and faithful: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\) for which the inequality holds for every finite nonnegative probability vector \(w\).

However, the introductory paragraph already states that the proof is incomplete: it says the full classification would follow from Conjecture~\(\ref{conj:missing}\), and that this remains “the only essential gap.” This is a fatal issue for answer-readiness.

### 2. Coloring criterion and positive examples

The coloring criterion is correct. The random-coloring argument transfers a deterministic lower bound on heavy \(a\)-subsets of color classes to the Bernoulli\((a/b)\) selection model.

The reciprocal cases \(p=1/k\) and the \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\), cases are correctly derived.

Lemma~\(\ref{lem:pairs}\) appears valid. The deletion/counting argument is somewhat compressed but correct: the invariant is preserved, the counted pairs are distinct, and the terminal/early-stop counts give at least \(b-1\) good pairs for \(b\ge6\).

The \(p=1/2\) proof is correct: \(X\stackrel d=1-X\), so \(\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2]\), hence \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\).

The deterministic coloring counterexample at \(a/b=3/10\) is correct: seven loads \(1/7\) and three zero loads give only \(\binom73=35\) heavy triples, below the required \(\binom92=36\). I cross-checked this numerically.

### 3. Multiplicative closure and topological closure

The multiplicative closure proposition is valid. The representation \(v_i=x_i y_i\), with \(x_i\sim\Ber(p)\), \(y_i\sim\Ber(q)\), gives independent Bernoulli\((pq)\) variables, and the conditional normalization argument is sound.

The closedness proof for \(\mathcal G\) is also valid. The finite set of subset sums is handled correctly in the two one-sided subsequence cases.

The base-interval corollary is correct: proving the inequality on \([1/6,1/3]\), together with \(1/2\in\mathcal G\) and multiplicative closure, would imply the full interval \([0,1/3]\).

But this is conditional, not a proof of the interval.

### 4. Negative direction

The negative examples are correct and complete for

\[
(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1).
\]

For \(1/3<p<1/2\), three equal weights require at least two successes and give probability \(3p^2-2p^3<p\). For \(1/2<p<1\), two equal weights require two successes and give probability \(p^2<p\).

Thus the negative direction is fully proved.

### 5. Small-\(p\) assertion and sharpness examples

This is the fatal section. The desired positive result for \(0\le p\le1/3\) is introduced as a conjecture:

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

The sharpness examples are correct, including the pivot-plus-dust construction. But examples showing sharpness do not prove the conjecture.

### 6. Unit-gap, homogeneous unit-gap, and centered two-tail forms

The equivalence between Conjecture~\(\ref{conj:missing}\) and UG is correct.

The homogeneous form HUG is correctly obtained by scaling.

The new centered two-tail comparison CTC is also correct. But it is only an equivalent restatement. No proof of CTC is given.

The subcritical shifted HUG proposition is valid as far as it goes. The induction is sound. However, as the draft itself says, it only proves HUG in the regime \(B\le qh/p\), which does not include the hard pivot normalization needed for the full conjecture.

Thus this section still leaves the central theorem unproved.

### 7. Cyclic, sequential, complement, pivot, and convex-hull reformulations

The rational cyclic formulation is correct. For a fixed cyclic interval, each original coefficient lands in that interval with probability \(r/b\), independently across coefficients, so the load has the desired Bernoulli distribution. The equivalence with the averaged inequality is valid.

The sequential identity is correct:

\[
\Pr[S_m<p]
=1-p\sum_i\Pr[p-w_i\le S_{i-1}<p].
\]

The complement formulation is correct: replacing \(v_i\) by \(1-v_i\) turns the desired lower-tail inequality into the strict upper-tail bound

\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q.
\]

The pivot inequality is correctly shown equivalent to the small-\(p\) assertion.

The convex-hull equivalence is essentially correct. The separation argument is a little terse, but the logic is sound: coordinatewise down-closedness of \(P(\mathcal D)\) allows separation from the upper orthant, producing a nonnegative separating vector.

The partition-function form follows correctly from \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=(1+\lambda)^{-m}Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)\).

The capped slice lemma remains explicitly unproved. It also still needs conventions for edge cases such as \(\mathcal F=\varnothing\) and \(\lambda=0\), but since it is only proposed as an induction target, this is not the main defect.

### 8. Coalescence identity and failed route

The coalescence identity is correct, including the endpoint conventions for the strict event. The \(p=3/10\), \(w_i=1/4\) example is also correct:

\[
\Phi_q(w)=6517/10000,\qquad
\Phi_q(w^{ij})=637/1000.
\]

I cross-checked these values. The general equal-weight obstruction is also correct.

This section is useful as a negative result about a possible proof strategy, but it does not prove the main theorem.

### 9. Remaining open issues

The “Remaining open issues” section accurately states the problem: the positive direction for all \(p\in[0,1/3]\) remains unproved. This section alone makes the document non-answer-ready.

---

## Previous concerns: status after revision

### Addressed or improved

- The deterministic coloring criterion is now clearly presented as only a sufficient condition, and the \(3/10\) counterexample is correctly used to show why it cannot prove all rational cases.
- The new centered two-tail formulation is mathematically correct and may be a useful reduction.
- The draft is honest about the remaining gap.

### Still unresolved

- The core assertion \([0,1/3]\subseteq\mathcal G\) remains unproved.
- The capped slice lemma remains unproved.
- The Fokkink–Meester–Pelekis results are still not enough to imply the needed near-hypotenuse strict-tail bound.
- Computational evidence for small down-sets is still only evidence, not a proof.
- The document still concludes with open issues instead of a complete classification.

### New issues introduced

No new fatal mathematical error appears in the revised `answer.tex`. The new CTC paragraph is correct. However, it may give an impression of progress while still being only an equivalent formulation of the same missing theorem.

The revised `research_notes.tex` appears to contain material after an `\end{document}` marker, so that later material would be ignored if that notes file were compiled. Since `research_notes.tex` is explicitly background-only and not the deliverable, this does not affect `answer.tex`.

---

## Final assessment

The revised draft contains many correct partial results and reductions. The negative direction is complete, and several positive families are rigorously proved. But the full classification is not proved, because the entire interval \(0\le p\le1/3\) remains conditional on an unproved conjecture.

Therefore `answer.tex` does not fully solve the stated problem.

### Workflow compile/format feedback ###
No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

### Advisory Council replies (if you requested any last turn) ###
### Council member: gpt-55-pro (models/openai/gpt-55-pro) ###
I would prioritize two avenues over further pointwise subharmonic attempts.

**1. Last-exit martingale/local-time proof.**  
Let \(M_k=\sum_{i\le k} b_i(\zeta_i-p)\) and \(I=(-q,p)\). Since upward jumps are \(\le q\) and downward jumps are \(\le p\), a path cannot jump directly from the upper exterior \([p,\infty)\) to the lower exterior \((-\infty,-q)\), or conversely; it must re-enter \(I\). Thus the final side is the side of the **last exit** from \(I\). Writing last-exit probabilities gives
\[
P_R=\sum_k p\,\mathbb E\big[1_{\{M_{k-1}\in[p-qb_k,p)\}}\,R_k(M_{k-1}+qb_k)\big],
\]
\[
P_L=\sum_k q\,\mathbb E\big[1_{\{M_{k-1}\in[-q,-q+pb_k)\}}\,L_k(M_{k-1}-pb_k)\big],
\]
where \(R_k,L_k\) are future “no-return then end right/left” probabilities. In CTC the prefactors become \(qP_R\) and \(pP_L\), so both have coefficient \(pq\); the issue is comparing occupation of an upper strip of width \(qb_k\) with a lower strip of width \(pb_k\). This exactly neutralizes the one-step failure near \(-q\). I would try to prove the needed comparison by optional stopping for the future Dirichlet functions \(R_k,L_k\), or via a discrete Tanaka formula. The relevant kernel is
\[
\mathbb E[(x+b_k(\zeta-p)-t)_+]-(x-t)_+
=
\begin{cases}
q(pb_k+u),& -pb_k<u<0,\\
p(qb_k-u),& 0\le u<qb_k,
\end{cases}
\quad u=t-x.
\]
This may yield a short “boundary local time” proof.

**2. Bellman, but with boundary credit.**  
The scalar \(g\) fails because it charges the lower boundary immediately. Instead define the finite-horizon value
\[
V(B,x)=\inf_{\sum c_i=B,\;0\le c_i\le1}
\mathbb E\,g\!\left(x+\sum c_i(\zeta_i-p)\right).
\]
The subcritical induction proves \(V(B,x)\ge0\) in one face of the \((B,x)\)-plane. I would numerically solve this Bellman recursion and look for a piecewise-linear certificate \(H(B,x)\le V(B,x)\) with \(H(B,0)=0\). The needed extra term should be a triangular local-time credit on the two boundary strips, not a harmonic linear correction.

**3. Convex-hull/partition route via capped support duality.**  
Your capped slice lemma looks very promising. I would attack it through the LP dual
\[
\rho^\lambda_{\mathcal E}(c)
=
\min_{\alpha_i\ge0}
\left\{\lambda\sum_i\alpha_i+
\max_{A\in\mathcal E}\sum_{i\in A}(c_i-\alpha_i)\right\}.
\]
This converts the cap \(x_i\le\lambda\) into penalties \(\alpha_i\). The hope is then to reduce the hypothesis
\[
\rho^\lambda_{\mathcal E}(c)+\lambda r_{\mathcal F}(c)<\lambda c([n])
\]
to a weighted cross-union condition to which Ahlswede–Daykin four-functions or a biased edge-isoperimetric argument applies. This is probably the cleanest combinatorial route to a self-contained proof.

**4. Extremal halfspace reduction.**  
Minimize the CTC deficit over coefficients. Since the objective is chamberwise constant, a strict minimizer should lie on many subset-sum hyperplanes \(Y=p\) or \(Y=-q\), or on \(b_i\in\{0,1\}\). A perturbation/KKT argument may force an extremal counterexample to have only unit coefficients plus at most one fractional block. That residual case reduces to binomial tail ratios and is checkable.

I would pursue (1) and (3) first; they seem most structurally aligned with the exact constants.

### Council member: opus_47_max (models/anthropic/opus_47_max) ###
Here are several angles I think could close the gap, ordered by how short a proof they might give.

**1. Single-state Bellman/martingale potential is provably impossible — go multi-state.**

A sub-mean function $V:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ for the steps $(+qc$ w.p.\ $p$, $-pc$ w.p.\ $q)$, $c\in[0,1]$, must be convex: Taylor expand the submartingale condition at $c=0$ to get $pq c^2 V''(\delta)/2 \ge O(c^3)$, hence $V''\ge0$. But a convex $V$ with $V\le g_0(\delta)=q\mathbf{1}_{\delta\le-p}-p\mathbf{1}_{\delta>q}$ and $V(0)\ge0$ forces $V\equiv0$ on $[-p,q]$ (convex above its tangent at $0$, sandwiched by $\le 0$), and then convexity pushes $V\ge 0$ on $\delta>q$, contradicting $V\le-p$ there. This explains why your subharmonic attempt for $g(y)$ near $-q$ is not fixable by adding a globally harmonic correction.

The natural escape is a **two-state** potential $V(y,v)$ where $v=\sum_{i\le k}b_i^2$ (running variance). The Taylor expansion at $c=0$ now gives $V_{yy}+2V_v\ge0$, i.e. $V$ is a subsolution of the backward heat equation. This is much weaker. A candidate: write $V(y,v)=g_0(y)\ast G_{v_\ast-v}(y)$ (convolution with a Gaussian of the "remaining" variance budget $v_\ast - v$), tweaked so $V(0,0)\ge0$. The Gaussian limit of CTC has slack $q/p$, so there is a positive margin to absorb the smoothing.

**2. Schur-convexity (reduction to binomial).**

Quick check at $p=1/3$: the CTC gap is $2/9$ at $(b_1,b_2)=(1,0)$ and $2/27$ at $(1/2,1/2)$ — i.e., moving toward equal coefficients (down in majorization) decreases the gap. If you can prove $f(b_1,\ldots,b_n)=q\Pr[Y\ge p]-p\Pr[Y<-q]$ is Schur-convex on $[0,1]^n$, the infimum over each "level set" $\sum b_i=B$ is at the most equal configuration, and the global infimum is at all-equal small coefficients — i.e., the Gaussian regime, where the inequality is trivially $q\Phi(-p/\sigma)\ge p\Phi(-q/\sigma)$ (Mills ratio is $\ge q/p>p/q$). Schur-convexity reduces to a Hessian-cone condition that's checkable directly on the two-coefficient slice, and after the Gaussian step you only need a clean finite check for "all $b_i\in\{0,1\}$" (unweighted binomial), where log-concavity of the binomial PMF gives strong margins. This is the route I'd try first for a short proof.

**3. Read León–Perron (2003) directly.**

The paper is already in your bib and its title is literally "Extremal Properties of Sums of Bernoulli Random Variables." Their machinery (sharp two-sided extremal probabilities for $\Pr[\sum c_i\xi_i\ge t]$ given moment constraints) is plausibly close enough to give CTC almost off the shelf, or to reduce it to a low-dimensional optimization. Combined with FMP's bold-play results (which cover neighboring rectangles but explicitly leave the hypotenuse), the published literature is unusually close to your missing case. Worth a careful re-read before manufacturing a new method.

**4. Two-shift inductive invariant.**

Your shifted induction (Prop.\ 7 in the draft) propagates positivity only on $\delta\in[0,qh-pB]$. Peeling a coefficient $c$ generates shifts $\delta+pc$ (stays subcritical) and $\delta-qc$ (can fall through). The "missing" piece is precisely a *mass* bound on $R$ in the strip $[T-h-pc,\,T-h)$. Try inducting on a *paired* invariant
$$F_R(\delta_+,\delta_-)\;=\;q\Pr[R\ge p(B+h)+\delta_+]\;-\;p\Pr[R<p(B+h)+\delta_--h]$$
where $(\delta_+,\delta_-)$ are coupled in a slightly larger region than the diagonal, and choose the coupling so the recursion closes simultaneously on both shifts when one would individually escape. The right "coupling region" is what your capped-replacement lemma is trying to encode in the support-function language — perhaps it is more natural in shift-coordinates than in convex-hull coordinates.

**5. Compressions on the partition-function form.**

The equivalent statement "$p\mathbf{1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\Rightarrow Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)\le(1+\lambda)^{m-1}$" is purely combinatorial. Try Frankl-style $(i,j)$-compressions $A\mapsto A\cup\{i\}\setminus\{j\}$ ($i<j$). These preserve $|A|$ (hence $Z_\lambda$), preserve down-closedness if you compress carefully, and one can hope they preserve $p\mathbf{1}\notin P(\mathcal D)$. After full compression the family is a shifted/left-compressed down-set with very restricted structure (e.g., determined by a few thresholds), and $Z_\lambda$ at the extremals is exactly the dictatorship $(1+\lambda)^{m-1}$.

**6. A long shot: a flip/coupling proof of the size-bias identity.**

Recall $\Pr[W\ge p]-p=\mathbb{E}[(1-W)\mathbf{1}_{W\ge p}]-\mathbb{E}[W\mathbf{1}_{W<p}]$. For $p\le 1/3$ the "outside" weight $1-W\ge 2/3$ on the right tail, while $W<p$ is small on the left. A concrete injection from $\{I\in A,\,W<p\}$ to $\{I\notin A,\,W\ge p\}$ (where $I$ is the size-biased index) — possibly via flipping the index Bernoulli plus a small rearrangement of the rest — would give the result. Your worked counterexamples rule out the naive injection but not a slightly cleverer one that exploits $p\le 1/3$.

**Uncertainty notes.** I have not verified the Schur-convexity (2) past one toy example; it could fail at a non-symmetric configuration. I have not opened the León–Perron paper to confirm (3) actually contains the hypotenuse case. The two-shift invariant in (4) is informed speculation about what your subcritical proof needs. The obstruction to a 1D scalar subharmonic proof (like your $g(y)$ test function) is exactly that it forgets "remaining step capacity"—near $-q$, a jump might overshoot the threshold, but the 1D function cannot credit this without knowing the remaining mass. 

Here are three distinct, rigorous avenues I recommend exploring to close the gap, leveraging structures you have already unearthed:

### 1. The Continuous-Time Empirical Martingale
Your random weighted quantile observation $Y(t) = \sum w_i \mathbf{1}_{U_i \le t}$ hides a massive structural advantage: it can be transformed into a classic empirical martingale. 
Specifically, the process 
$$ M_t = \frac{Y(t) - t}{1-t} $$
is a continuous-time martingale on $t \in [0, 1)$ with respect to the standard filtration $\mathcal{F}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{1}_{U_i \le s} : s \le t)$. 
You want to prove $\Pr(M_p \ge 0) \ge p$.

Notice how beautifully this aligns with your thresholds: at $t=p$, the absolute lowest possible value of $M_p$ (if all $U_i > p$) is exactly $-p/(1-p) = -p/q$. This perfectly mirrors the $-q$ lower bound in your (CTC) formulation! By embedding the discrete steps into a sum of independent continuous-time jump martingales, you unlock tools from empirical processes. You can apply optional stopping at $\tau = \inf\{t : M_t \ge 0\}$, reducing the problem to bounding the positive-excursion measure of a mean-zero jump martingale. 

### 2. 2D Bellman / State Augmentation
To fix the failure of $g(y)$ near $-q$, your induction state must track the remaining available step size. You already noted the Homogeneous Unit Gap (HUG) formulation explicitly carries the gap parameter $h$. Instead of searching for a 1D subharmonic function, formulate a 2D Bellman function $V(y, h)$ (or $V(y, B)$). 

Literature parallel: Bentkus (2004) and Pinelis (2016) handle asymmetric left-tail domination exactly this way—they carry the variance or total mass as a second dimension. This allows the value function to be locally smooth and curved in $y$ (fixing the zero-gradient issue), while absorbing the boundary conditions along the $h$ axis. A successful $V$ would satisfy $V(y, 0) \le q \mathbf{1}_{y \ge p} - p \mathbf{1}_{y < -q}$, with the 2D jump condition $q V(y - pc, h - c) + p V(y + qc, h - c) \ge V(y, h)$.

However, by LP duality (or max-flow min-cut on the hypercube), your desired bound $q \mu_p(Y \ge p) \ge p \mu_p(Y < -q)$ is exactly equivalent to the existence of a measure-preserving directed flow from the lower-tail configurations to the upper-tail ones.

## 1. Literature conclusion

I did not find a known theorem that proves CTC as stated.

The closest exact framework remains Fokkink--Meester--Pelekis, *Optimizing stakes in simultaneous bets*, ALEA 20 (2023), 153--165.  They define
\[
  \pi(p,t)=\sup_{\gamma}\Pr\!\left(\sum_i c_i\beta_i\ge t\right),
\]
over convex combinations of iid Bernoulli(\(p\)) variables.  In complement notation, CTC/original small-\(p\) would follow from the strict hypotenuse theorem
\[
  \pi(q,t)\le q\qquad(q\ge2/3,\ t>q). \tag{FMP-strip}
\]
Indeed, for fixed finite weights \(w_i\) with \(\sum_iw_i=1\), the event
\[
  \sum_iw_i\eta_i>q,\qquad \eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(q),
\]
equals \(\sum_iw_i\eta_i\ge t_*\), where \(t_*\) is the smallest attainable subset sum strictly larger than \(q\).  Thus (FMP-strip) gives \(\Pr(\sum_iw_i\eta_i>q)\le q\), equivalently
\(\Pr(\sum_iw_iv_i\ge p)\ge p\).  The pivot/unit-gap equivalence in the draft then gives CTC.

But FMP does **not** prove (FMP-strip).  Their Theorem 4.4 proves bold play when
\[
  {k\over k+1}<p\le {k+1\over k+2}<t,
\]
and Proposition 4.5 proves diagonal endpoints \(p=t=(k+1)/(k+2)\) for \(k>1\).  The needed region is exactly the leftover near-hypotenuse strip
\[
  q<t\le {k+1\over k+2},\qquad {k\over k+1}<q<{k+1\over k+2}.
\]
FMP explicitly say an array of triangles along the hypotenuse remains open.

Other checked areas:

- Fishburn--Frankl--Freed--Lagarias--Odlyzko intersecting-system results, Frankl/Tokushige/Filmus biased-intersection theorems: useful for reciprocal/endpoints, but not enough above bias \(2/3\) without the extra weighted-threshold structure.
- Pinelis left-tail domination: sharp moment-based left-tail bounds, but does not compare the two CTC tails with factor \(p/q\), and moment-only data are too weak near pivot-plus-dust.
- Bentkus/Dzindzalieta: sharp symmetric/Rademacher comparisons, structurally mismatched because CTC increments are asymmetric \((+qb_i,-pb_i)\).
- Samuels/Feige: adjacent small-deviation extremal problems, but I found no implication to the strict convex-combination Bernoulli hypotenuse.
- Kellerer: still no finite-cube down-set convex-hull or Bernoulli halfspace inequality found.

g(y)=q1_{\{y\ge p\}}-p1_{\{y<-q\}},

Negative values appear, so an \((x,R)\)-only Bellman proof would prove a false adaptive statement.

def is_down(D,n):

    for S in list(D):
        T=S
        sub=T
        u=sub
        # iterate subsets
        sub=S
        t=sub
        x=S

        # standard
        U=S
        sub=U
        while True:

            sub=(sub-1)&S
    return True
def compress_ij(D,n,i,j): # i,j 0-index, move j to i
    D=set(D)
    new=set(D)
    for S in list(D):
        if (S>>j)&1 and not ((S>>i)&1):
            T=(S & ~(1<<j)) | (1<<i)
            if T not in D:
                new.remove(S); new.add(T)
    return new

# enumerate downsets n=4, p=1/3 and check preservation

    for mask in range(1<<(1<<n)):

from pathlib import Path
ans_path=Path('/mnt/data/file-85uXLhoqjM5ShCKe85sJwi-answer.tex')
ans=ans_path.read_text()
insert_after = r"""\[
 p{\bf 1}\notin P(\mathcal D)
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 \sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}
 \le (1+\lambda)^{m-1},
 \qquad 0\le\lambda\le\frac12 .                         \tag{4}
\]
"""
compression = r"""
One can at least reduce (4) to shifted down-sets.  For \(i<j\), the
\((i,j)\)-compression replaces a set \(A\) with
\((A\setminus\{j\})\cup\{i\}\) when \(j\in A\), \(i\notin A\), and the
replacement is not already present.

\begin{proposition}[shifted reduction]\label{prop:shifted}
If (4) holds for shifted down-sets, then it holds for all down-sets.
More precisely, an \((i,j)\)-compression preserves the partition function
\(Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)=\sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}\) and also preserves
the obstruction \(p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The preservation of \(Z_\lambda\) is standard, since compression preserves
cardinalities.  It remains to prove the convex-hull assertion.  We argue
contrapositively.  Suppose \(p{\bf1}\in P(C\mathcal D)\), where
\(C\mathcal D\) is the \((i,j)\)-compression.  Thus there is a probability
measure \(\nu\) on \(C\mathcal D\) with every coordinate marginal equal to
\(p\).

Fix the other coordinates \(R\subseteq[m]\setminus\{i,j\}\).  The only points
of \(C\mathcal D\) which may fail to lie in \(\mathcal D\) are moved points
\(R\cup\{i\}\) for which \(R\cup\{j\}\in\mathcal D\) but
\(R\cup\{i\}\notin\mathcal D\).  Move all \(\nu\)-mass on such a point back to
\(R\cup\{j\}\).  This keeps all marginals except possibly those of \(i\) and
\(j\).  If the total moved mass is \(M\), then the \(j\)-marginal now exceeds
the \(i\)-marginal by \(2M\).

Before this move, the total \(\nu\)-mass on \(i\)-only points equalled the
total \(\nu\)-mass on \(j\)-only points, because the \(i\)- and \(j\)-marginals
of \(\nu\) were equal.  The \(i\)-only mass included the moved mass \(M\), while
any \(j\)-only point still present in \(C\mathcal D\) has its \(i\)-only mate in
\(\mathcal D\) as well; otherwise it would have been compressed away.  Hence
there is at least \(M\) mass on such swappable \(j\)-only points.  Move mass
\(M\) from them to their \(i\)-only mates.  The support is now contained in
\(\mathcal D\), all other marginals were unchanged, and the \(i\)- and
\(j\)-marginals have been restored to \(p\).  Therefore
\(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal D)\).
\end{proof}
"""

\section*{Round 17: compression and shifted-complex route}

A useful exact reduction for the convex-hull/partition-function formulation is
standard shifting.  Let \(\mathcal D\) be a down-set and \(C_{ij}\mathcal D\)
its \((i,j)\)-compression, \(i<j\).  Compression preserves all set sizes and
therefore \(Z_\lambda\).  It also preserves the obstruction
\(p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\).  Contrapositive proof: assume
\(p{\bf1}\in P(C_{ij}\mathcal D)\), with a probability measure \(\nu\) on the
compressed family having all marginals \(p\).  Work in each diamond
\(R,R\cup\{i\},R\cup\{j\},R\cup\{i,j\}\).  The only compressed sets possibly
not in the original family are moved \(i\)-only points \(R\cup\{i\}\) whose
preimage \(R\cup\{j\}\) lies in \(\mathcal D\).  Move all mass on these points
back to their \(j\)-only preimages; if total moved mass is \(M\), the
\(j\)-marginal exceeds the \(i\)-marginal by \(2M\).  Initially the total
\(i\)-only and \(j\)-only masses were equal.  Since a \(j\)-only point still
present after compression has its \(i\)-only mate in \(\mathcal D\), there is at
least \(M\) mass on swappable \(j\)-only points whose mates both lie in
\(\mathcal D\).  Move \(M\) of that mass to the corresponding \(i\)-only mates.
All non-\(i,j\) marginals are unchanged and the \(i,j\) marginals are restored
to \(p\).  This gives a measure on \(\mathcal D\) with marginals \(p\).

Therefore the missing inequality
\[
  p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\Longrightarrow
  Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)\le(1+\lambda)^{n-1},\quad
  \lambda=p/(1-p)\le1/2
\]
may be sought only among shifted down-sets.

Possible shifted induction.  For a shifted down-set on \([n+1]\), write
\[
 \mathcal D=\mathcal E\cup\{A\cup\{n+1\}:A\in\mathcal F\},
 \qquad \mathcal F\subseteq\mathcal E.
\]
Shiftedness gives more than \(\mathcal F\subseteq\mathcal E\): if
\(A\in\mathcal F\) and \(j\notin A\), then \(A\cup\{j\}\in\mathcal E\), i.e.
\(\mathcal E\) contains the full upper shadow of \(\mathcal F\).  The convex
condition recurses as follows:
\[
 p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal D)
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
 \exists x\in P(\mathcal F),\ y\in P(\mathcal E)
 \text{ with } p x+q y=p{\bf1},
\]
or equivalently \(y=\lambda({\bf1}-x)\), \(\lambda=p/q\).
If \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)>q\), then \(\mu_p(\mathcal E)+\lambda\mu_p(\mathcal F)>1\).
By induction, if both \(\mathcal E\) and \(\mathcal F\) fail to contain
\(p{\bf1}\) in their convex hulls, then
\(\mu_p(\mathcal E),\mu_p(\mathcal F)\le q\), whence
\(\mu_p(\mathcal E)+\lambda\mu_p(\mathcal F)\le q(1+\lambda)=1\), a
contradiction.  If \(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal F)\), then taking
\(x=y=p{\bf1}\) proves \(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal D)\).  Thus the only genuinely
mixed case is
\[
 p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal E),\qquad p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal F),
 \qquad \mu_p(\mathcal E)+\lambda\mu_p(\mathcal F)>1.
\]
This is exactly where the capped-slice lemma enters; shiftedness may make the
shadow containment strong enough to build \(x\in P(\mathcal F)\) with
\(\lambda({\bf1}-x)\in P(\mathcal E)\).

Small shifted-complex sanity checks.  Exhaustive enumeration through \(n=5\)
confirms the shifted reduction and finds no shifted counterexample for
\(p=1/4,0.3,1/3\).  The largest shifted non-convex examples are the expected
dictatorships, e.g. all subsets missing the last coordinate, with measure
\(q\).  The next examples are lower by a visible gap; for \(n=5,p=0.3\), a
typical next extremal has facets all three-subsets of the first four vertices
and measure \(0.69433<0.7\).

Tail-rank inequalities alone do not characterize \(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal D)\)
for shifted complexes.  Example on five vertices with facets
\[
 12,\ 13,\ 23,\ 4,\ 5
\]
(in shifted notation) has tail ranks satisfying
\(r(T)\ge p|T|\) for \(p=0.3\), but \(p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\).  A
separating vector is proportional to
\[
 (1,1,1,2,2),
\]
for which every facet has weight \(2/7<0.3\cdot 1\) after normalization.
Its \(p\)-biased measure at \(p=0.3\) is \(0.62083<0.7\), so it is not close to
a counterexample.  This shows that a shifted proof needs more than tail-rank
checks; arbitrary nondecreasing separating weights still matter.

Literature/history note.  The exact problem appeared on Math StackExchange in
2012 under the title ``Convex Combinations of Low Probability Bernoulli
Variables''; the posted counterexamples there start above \(1/3\), and no
counterexample below \(1/3\) was found.  A 2017 MathOverflow question by Ron P.
asked for constant lower bounds for convex combinations of iid Bernoulli
variables; Tao supplied general lower bounds, but not the sharp \(p\)-lower
bound considered here.  A comment there cites Liggett's EKR-based theorem in
the threshold \(1/2\) direction; the relevant statement is an upper bound for
low-bias upper tails of intersecting weighted threshold families, not a proof of
the present small-\(p\) lower-tail assertion.  Fokkink--Meester--Pelekis (2023)
remain the closest published framework via the bold-play function \(\pi(p,t)\),
but their theorems do not cover the near-hypotenuse strict strip needed here.

\end{document}
"""
